transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Sydney, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about my stomach issues lately. It’s just... I don’t know, it feels like it’s never-ending. I mean, I try to follow what the doctors say, but sometimes I wonder if it’s really going to get better. It’s just hard to shake off that feeling, you know? Like, what if there’s something else going on that they’re not seeing?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds really frustrating to be dealing with these ongoing stomach issues, especially when you’re trying to follow the doctors' advice. I hear a sense of uncertainty and worry about whether things will improve or if there might be something more serious that hasn’t been addressed. Can you tell me more about what those feelings are like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Yeah, it’s just... I feel like I’m stuck in this loop, you know? I mean, I try to stay positive and think that maybe it’s just a phase or something, but then I get these thoughts creeping in. Like, what if it’s something really serious? And then I start to feel anxious, and it just spirals. I guess I’m just worried that I’m not doing enough to help myself, even though I’m trying to follow everything. It’s exhausting, honestly.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re caught in a difficult cycle of trying to stay positive while also grappling with those anxious thoughts about your health. That feeling of being stuck and exhausted must be really heavy to carry. When you think about those worries, what kind of thoughts come up for you, and how do they affect your day-to-day life?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, it’s like... I keep thinking about all the things I might be doing wrong, you know? Like, am I eating the right foods? Should I be exercising more? Or maybe I’m just overthinking everything. It’s hard to focus on anything else when my stomach is acting up. Sometimes, I just want to ignore it and pretend it’s not there, but then it feels like it’s always in the back of my mind. I guess it affects how I interact with people too. I don’t want to be that person who’s always talking about their health issues, but it’s hard not to when it’s consuming so much of my thoughts.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re really grappling with a lot of self-doubt and pressure around your health, which is understandable given how much it occupies your mind. I hear that you’re trying to balance wanting to be present with others while also feeling overwhelmed by your stomach issues. How do you think this struggle impacts your relationships or how you feel when you’re with other people?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, it’s tough, you know? I feel like I’m always holding back. Like, I want to engage and be present, but then I worry that if I bring it up, people will think I’m just complaining. So, I end up just nodding along or changing the subject, which probably makes me seem distant or uninterested. It’s frustrating because I don’t want my health to define me, but it’s hard to shake that feeling. Sometimes I think maybe I should just be more open about it, but then I get scared of how people will react. It’s like I’m stuck between wanting to connect and wanting to hide away.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re navigating a really challenging balance between wanting to connect with others and the fear of being perceived as complaining or distant. That internal conflict must be really tough to manage. When you think about being more open with people about your health, what feelings or thoughts come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: I guess I feel a mix of things. There’s this part of me that thinks it might be nice to share what I’m going through, like maybe it would help me feel less alone. But then there’s this nagging voice that says people won’t really understand or care, you know? I worry they’ll just see me as someone who’s always focused on their problems. It’s like, I don’t want to burden anyone with my issues, but at the same time, it feels isolating to keep it all bottled up. It’s just a lot to juggle, and I’m not sure how to find that balance.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re feeling torn between the desire for connection and the fear of being a burden to others. That mix of wanting to share your experience but also worrying about how it will be received can create a lot of inner tension. It’s understandable to feel isolated when you’re holding so much in. Can you share more about what you think might help you feel more comfortable opening up, or what that balance might look like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I think maybe it would help if I found the right moment or setting to talk about it, you know? Like, if I could feel that the other person is really listening and open to it, then maybe I wouldn’t feel so scared. But then again, I also worry that I might just end up rambling or making it awkward. I guess I just want to make sure that it’s a two-way conversation, not just me unloading all my worries. It’s tricky because I don’t want to come off as needy or like I’m looking for sympathy. I just want to feel like I’m being honest without it becoming this heavy thing. It’s a lot to think about, honestly.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re really considering the importance of the right environment and mutual understanding when it comes to sharing your feelings. Wanting it to be a two-way conversation shows how much you value connection and authenticity. I can see how that would make it feel less daunting. What do you think would help you identify those moments or settings where you feel more comfortable opening up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I guess it might help if I pay more attention to the vibe of the conversation, you know? Like, if I notice that someone seems genuinely interested or if they’re sharing their own experiences, that could be a good sign. I think I need to trust my instincts a bit more, but it’s hard when I’m in my head so much. Maybe I could also practice bringing it up in smaller doses, just to test the waters. But then again, I worry that even that might come off as forced or awkward. It’s just... I don’t know, I feel like I’m overthinking it all again. It’s like I want to be open, but I keep second-guessing myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re really trying to navigate this with a lot of thoughtfulness, wanting to trust your instincts while also feeling the weight of overthinking. It’s understandable to feel hesitant when you’re trying to find that balance between being open and not wanting to come off as forced. When you think about practicing those smaller doses of sharing, what do you imagine that might look like for you in a conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I guess it could start with something simple, like mentioning how I’ve been feeling a bit off lately, you know? Just a casual comment, not diving into all the details right away. Maybe if the other person responds positively, I could share a bit more about what’s been going on. I think it would help to keep it light at first, just to see how they react. But then I get stuck on whether I should even bring it up at all. It’s like, what if they don’t really want to hear it? I don’t want to make things uncomfortable. It’s just... I don’t know, it feels like a balancing act, and I’m not sure I’m doing it right.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re envisioning a gradual approach, starting with something light and gauging the other person's response before diving deeper. That makes a lot of sense, especially when you’re trying to find a comfortable way to share. The uncertainty about whether others want to hear it can add to that balancing act. When you think about those moments of hesitation, what do you think might be at the root of that concern?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I think a lot of it comes down to fear, honestly. Fear of rejection or judgment, you know? Like, what if they don’t care or they just brush it off? It makes me hesitate because I don’t want to put myself out there and feel vulnerable, only to have it not be received well. And then there’s this worry that if I do share, I might end up opening up a can of worms, and then it becomes this whole thing. I guess I just want to protect myself from feeling hurt or embarrassed. It’s like I’m constantly weighing the risks of being honest against the comfort of keeping things to myself. It’s exhausting, really.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like fear of rejection and judgment is a significant factor in your hesitation to open up, and that makes a lot of sense. Wanting to protect yourself from potential hurt or embarrassment can create a heavy burden to carry. It’s understandable to feel exhausted from weighing those risks. When you think about the possibility of being vulnerable, what do you think it would take for you to feel safe enough to take that step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: You know, I think it would take a lot of trust, honestly. Like, I’d need to feel that the person I’m talking to is genuinely there for me, not just listening to respond but really trying to understand. Maybe if I had some reassurance that they wouldn’t judge me or think less of me for sharing, that could help. I guess it’s also about feeling like it’s a safe space, where I won’t be dismissed or have my feelings minimized. But even then, there’s still that nagging voice in my head that says it might not go well, and that makes it hard to let my guard down. It’s just... I don’t know, it feels like I’m always walking this tightrope between wanting to connect and fearing the fall.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like trust and a sense of safety are really crucial for you when it comes to opening up. Wanting to feel genuinely understood and reassured can create a foundation for connection. I can see how that tightrope walk between wanting to connect and fearing the potential fallout can be so challenging. When you think about the people in your life, are there any relationships where you feel a bit more trust or safety to share?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, there are a couple of friends I feel somewhat comfortable with, but it’s still tricky. I mean, I can talk to them about lighter stuff, and they seem to care, but when it comes to the deeper issues, I still hold back. I guess I worry that if I start sharing too much, it might change the dynamic of our friendship. Like, what if they don’t know how to handle it or if it makes things awkward? It’s frustrating because I want to be able to lean on them, but then I get caught up in my head about how they might react. So, I end up keeping things surface-level, which feels safe but also leaves me feeling a bit disconnected. It’s just... I don’t know, it’s like I’m stuck in this loop where I want to reach out, but I keep pulling back.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re in a bit of a bind, wanting to reach out to your friends but feeling hesitant about how it might change the dynamic of your relationships. That desire for deeper connection is there, but the fear of potential awkwardness or misunderstanding keeps you from fully opening up. It’s understandable to feel frustrated by that loop. When you think about those moments of wanting to share more with your friends, what do you think holds you back the most?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: You know, I think it really comes down to that fear of vulnerability again. It’s like, I want to be honest and share what’s really going on, but then I start to imagine all the possible outcomes. What if they don’t know what to say? Or worse, what if they feel burdened by my problems? I don’t want to be the one who brings down the mood or makes things uncomfortable. It’s just... I feel like I have this internal checklist of things that could go wrong, and it makes me hesitate. I guess I just keep thinking about how I might be perceived, and that’s what really holds me back. It’s exhausting to think about it all the time, but I can’t seem to help it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that fear of vulnerability is really weighing on you, and the mental checklist of potential outcomes can be quite overwhelming. Wanting to avoid being a burden or changing the mood is a common concern, and it makes sense that it would hold you back from sharing more deeply. When you reflect on how you perceive yourself in those moments, what do you think contributes to that worry about how you might be seen by others?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I think a lot of it ties back to those core beliefs I have about myself, you know? Like, I often feel like I’m unlovable or that I’m just not enough. So, when I think about opening up, I can’t help but wonder if people will see me as weak or needy. It’s like I’m constantly battling this voice in my head that tells me I should just handle things on my own. I guess I fear that if I show my true self, they might not like what they see, and that just reinforces this feeling of being trapped in my own head. It’s frustrating because I want to connect, but then I get stuck in this cycle of self-doubt and worry about how I’ll be perceived. It’s like I’m my own worst critic, and that makes it hard to let anyone in.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like those core beliefs about being unlovable or not enough are really powerful for you, and they create a lot of internal conflict when it comes to opening up. It’s understandable that you would feel trapped in that cycle of self-doubt, especially when you want to connect but fear how you might be perceived. When you think about that inner critic, what do you notice about how it speaks to you or what it tells you in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Oh, it’s pretty relentless, honestly. It’s like this constant stream of negative thoughts that just doesn’t let up. It tells me things like, “You’re not worth their time,” or “They’ll think you’re just looking for attention.” It’s almost like it’s trying to protect me by keeping me from being vulnerable, but really, it just ends up isolating me more. I can hear it saying that I should just tough it out and not bother anyone with my problems. It’s exhausting to have that voice in my head, and sometimes I wonder if it’s just easier to keep everything bottled up instead of dealing with the fallout of being open. But then, I think about how lonely that can feel, and it’s like I’m stuck in this loop where I want to break free but don’t know how. It’s frustrating, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that inner critic is quite harsh and persistent, almost like it’s trying to shield you from vulnerability but ultimately ends up deepening your sense of isolation. I can see how that creates a frustrating cycle of wanting to connect but feeling trapped by those negative thoughts. When you think about the loneliness that comes from keeping everything bottled up, what emotions does that bring up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Honestly, it brings up a lot of sadness and frustration. I mean, there’s this deep sense of longing for connection, but then I just feel this weight of loneliness pressing down on me. It’s like I can see what I want—having those meaningful conversations and feeling understood—but I keep getting in my own way. Sometimes I feel angry at myself for not being able to just let go and reach out. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle where I want to break free, but I don’t know how to take that first step. And then there’s this nagging fear that if I do try to reach out, it might not go the way I hope, and that just makes me want to retreat even more. It’s exhausting to feel this way, and I wish I could find a way to just let myself be open without all the second-guessing.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you're carrying a heavy mix of sadness, frustration, and longing for connection, which makes it really difficult to take that first step toward openness. I can hear how much you desire those meaningful conversations, yet the fear of how it might turn out holds you back. It’s understandable to feel angry at yourself for feeling stuck in this cycle. When you think about what it would feel like to break free from that cycle, what do you imagine it might look like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: You know, breaking free from that cycle feels like it would be such a relief, honestly. I imagine it would be like finally taking a deep breath after holding it in for so long. I think it would mean being able to share my thoughts and feelings without that constant fear of judgment looming over me. Maybe I’d feel lighter, like I could actually connect with others instead of just skimming the surface. I picture having those deeper conversations where I don’t have to worry about how I’m perceived, just being able to be myself. But then, there’s that voice again, reminding me of all the risks involved, and it makes me hesitate. It’s like I can see the possibility of what could be, but the fear just keeps pulling me back. I guess I just want to feel that sense of freedom, but it feels so far away sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like the idea of breaking free feels incredibly liberating for you, like a breath of fresh air after holding your feelings in for so long. The vision of being able to share openly and connect deeply is so appealing, yet that persistent voice of fear keeps pulling you back. It’s understandable to feel torn between wanting that freedom and the risks that come with vulnerability. When you think about that fear and the risks it highlights, what do you think it’s trying to protect you from?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: I think that fear is trying to protect me from getting hurt, you know? It’s like it’s warning me about the potential for rejection or misunderstanding. I mean, I’ve had experiences in the past where I opened up and it didn’t go well, and I think that just reinforced this idea that being vulnerable is risky. So, that voice in my head is like a safety net, trying to keep me from feeling that pain again. But at the same time, I realize it’s also keeping me from experiencing the good stuff—like real connections and support. It’s frustrating because I know that not every situation will end badly, but that fear just feels so real and powerful. It’s like I’m stuck in this tug-of-war between wanting to protect myself and wanting to take a chance on connection.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that fear is really rooted in past experiences where vulnerability led to pain, and it’s trying to act as a protective mechanism for you. It’s understandable to want to shield yourself from potential rejection or misunderstanding, especially when those feelings feel so real. Yet, at the same time, you’re aware that this protection might be keeping you from the positive experiences of connection and support. When you think about those past experiences that reinforced this fear, what stands out to you the most?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, there are a few moments that really stick with me. I remember this one time in high school when I tried to open up to a friend about my anxiety. I thought it would help, you know? But instead of understanding, they just brushed it off and made a joke about it. It felt like I was being dismissed, and it made me feel so small and unimportant. That moment really stuck with me, and I think it’s part of why I hesitate to share now. It’s like that experience taught me that being vulnerable can lead to ridicule instead of support. And then there was another time in college when I confided in someone about my struggles with my weight. They didn’t mean to, but their reaction made me feel ashamed, like I was just looking for attention or something. It’s those kinds of moments that replay in my mind, and I can’t help but think that if I open up again, it might end up just as badly. It’s frustrating because I know not everyone will react that way, but those memories linger and make it hard to trust that things could be different.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like those experiences in high school and college were really painful for you, and they left a lasting impact on how you view vulnerability and sharing your feelings. It’s understandable that being dismissed or feeling ashamed would create a strong hesitation to open up again. Those moments can really shape our beliefs about ourselves and how we expect others to respond. When you think about those past experiences, how do you feel they influence your current relationships or your willingness to share with others now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Client: Sydney: You know, I think those experiences really cast a long shadow over my current relationships. It’s like I carry that fear with me, and it makes me hesitant to let anyone in. I find myself second-guessing everything I say, worried that I’ll be met with the same dismissal or judgment. So, I tend to keep things surface-level, which feels safe but also leaves me feeling disconnected. I mean, I want to be open and share my thoughts, but that fear of being misunderstood or ridiculed just looms large. It’s almost like I’m on high alert, waiting for the other shoe to drop. I can see how it affects my ability to form deeper connections, and it’s frustrating because I know I’m missing out on the kind of relationships I crave. But then, that voice in my head just keeps reminding me of those past hurts, and it’s hard to shake off. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle of wanting to connect but feeling like I have to protect myself at all costs.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like those past experiences have really shaped how you approach your current relationships, creating a sense of vigilance and fear that keeps you from opening up fully. I can hear how much you desire deeper connections, yet that protective instinct makes it hard to let your guard down. It must be exhausting to navigate that tension between wanting to connect and feeling the need to shield yourself. When you think about your current relationships, are there any moments where you’ve felt a glimmer of connection or openness, even if just a little?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Yeah, there have been a few moments that stand out, actually. I remember a time when I was hanging out with a couple of friends, and we started talking about our struggles with mental health. It felt different because everyone was really open, sharing their own experiences without judgment. I felt this little spark of connection, like we were all in the same boat, you know? It was refreshing to feel understood, even if just for a moment. But then, right after, I felt that familiar urge to retreat, like I shouldn’t have shared so much. It’s like I can see the potential for connection, but that fear creeps back in, making me doubt whether I should have let my guard down at all. It’s frustrating because I want to experience more of those moments, but I also feel like I’m constantly battling that voice that tells me to hold back.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like those moments of connection with your friends were really meaningful for you, providing a sense of understanding and shared experience. Yet, I can see how quickly that urge to retreat can overshadow those positive feelings, leaving you questioning whether you should have opened up at all. It’s understandable to feel that push and pull between wanting to connect and the fear of vulnerability. When you reflect on that spark of connection you felt, what do you think allowed that moment to happen, despite the fears you usually carry?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: You know, I think what allowed that moment to happen was the atmosphere we were in. It felt safe, like everyone was really there for each other, and it was almost like a collective understanding that we were all struggling in some way. I think seeing my friends being so open made me feel like it was okay to share, too. It was like this unspoken agreement that we could be real with each other without judgment. But then, as soon as I let my guard down a little, that voice came back, reminding me of all the risks. It’s frustrating because I want to feel that connection more often, but it’s like I have to remind myself that those moments can exist, even if they’re fleeting. I guess it just makes me wonder how I can create more of those safe spaces for myself, but then I get caught up in the fear of whether I can really trust it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like the safe atmosphere and the openness of your friends really played a crucial role in allowing you to share in that moment. It’s wonderful that you were able to experience that sense of collective understanding, even if it was brief. I can see how that makes you long for more of those safe spaces, yet the fear of trusting those moments still lingers. When you think about what it would take for you to feel more comfortable creating those safe spaces for yourself, what comes to mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I think it would take a lot of intentionality on my part, you know? Like, I’d have to actively seek out those environments where I feel supported and understood. Maybe it means being more selective about who I spend time with or even initiating those deeper conversations myself, which is a bit daunting. I guess I’d need to remind myself that not everyone will react like those past experiences, and that there are people out there who are willing to listen and be supportive. It’s just hard to shake off that fear of judgment, and I worry that if I put myself out there, I might end up feeling vulnerable and exposed again. But I do want to try, even if it feels like a leap of faith. I think creating those spaces would help me feel more connected, but it’s just figuring out how to take that first step without letting the fear take over.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re recognizing the need for intentionality in seeking out supportive environments and being more selective about your connections. It’s great that you’re open to the idea of initiating deeper conversations, even though it feels daunting. I can hear how much you want to take that leap of faith, yet the fear of vulnerability and judgment still feels like a significant barrier. When you think about taking that first step, what do you think might help you feel more grounded or supported as you navigate those feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: Well, I think having a plan could help me feel more grounded. Like, if I could outline what I want to say or even practice it a bit beforehand, it might ease some of that anxiety. I also wonder if having a trusted friend with me during those conversations could provide a sense of support, you know? Just knowing that someone has my back might make it feel less intimidating. And maybe I could remind myself of those moments when I felt connected, like the one with my friends, to help counteract that voice of doubt. It’s just tough because I know how quickly that fear can creep back in, and I don’t want to sabotage myself before I even start. But I guess if I take it one step at a time and focus on the potential for connection rather than the fear, it might make it a little easier.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like having a plan and practicing what you want to say could really help you feel more prepared and grounded. I can see how having a trusted friend with you would provide that extra layer of support, making those conversations feel less intimidating. It’s also insightful that you want to remind yourself of those positive moments of connection to counterbalance the fear. Taking it one step at a time and focusing on the potential for connection is a valuable approach. As you think about these strategies, what do you feel might be the biggest challenge for you in implementing them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: You know, I think the biggest challenge for me would be actually overcoming that initial hesitation to reach out. It’s like there’s this mental block that kicks in, and I start second-guessing myself before I even make a move. I might think, “What if they don’t want to hear what I have to say?” or “What if I end up feeling embarrassed again?” It’s frustrating because I can see the potential for a good conversation, but that fear just looms over me. I also worry about how I’ll handle it if the conversation doesn’t go as I hope. I guess it’s that fear of the unknown that really holds me back. I want to push through it, but it feels like I’m constantly battling that voice that tells me to play it safe. It’s exhausting, honestly.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that initial hesitation and the mental block can feel really overwhelming, especially with those thoughts of doubt and fear of embarrassment creeping in. It’s understandable to feel frustrated when you see the potential for connection but feel held back by that fear of the unknown. I can hear how exhausting it is to navigate that internal battle. When you think about those moments of doubt, what do you think might help you challenge or quiet that voice that tells you to play it safe?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Sydney: You know, I think one thing that might help is trying to reframe those thoughts when they pop up. Like, instead of letting that voice dictate my actions, I could remind myself that it’s okay to feel nervous, but it doesn’t mean I shouldn’t try. Maybe I could even write down some positive affirmations or reminders about the times I’ve connected with others successfully. It might help to have something tangible to look at when I’m feeling that doubt creeping in. I also wonder if talking through those fears with someone I trust could help me gain some perspective. It’s just tough because, in the moment, it feels so real and overwhelming. I guess I need to find ways to remind myself that taking risks can lead to good things, even if it feels scary at first. But, you know, it’s easier said than done.

While the question did not completely shift the client's perspective or challenge their assumptions to the extent of leading to a major breakthrough, it did facilitate a deeper level of self-reflection and exploration of their emotional state.
